A Restore Forward gathering

WILD! HealingA harvest of medicine

A refusal to surrender our bodies — and an invitation to walk back and get what was lost.

Ava, New York & Online

Online 9 Sep Online 10 Sep Online 11 Sep On the land 12 Sep
Get tickets → Read the invitation
The remembering

In the days of old, when a person lost their way, the elders would say: "Go back to the forest. Walk with the medicines. Listen again."

WILD! Healing is a return to that ancient remembering. It is not simply a program. It is a pilgrimage — a medicine walk across time and continents, guided by healers, farmers, herbalists, and wisdom keepers who still remember how to call the body back to life. Across two days of virtual immersion, you travel a living landscape of ancestral healing, culminating in a third day on the land: a glorious feast and festival in the woods, where the community celebrates the miracle of healing and honors those who heal the healers.

Why now

A refusal to surrender our bodies

We have all been witness — to the poisoning of our food and water, to the slow violence of a world that forgot how sacred the body is, to the diseases awakened inside us.

And still, you are here. Still breathing. Still searching for a path back to life. WILD! Healing gathers international healers, farmers, herbalists, and physicians not to dominate illness, but to outnumber it with wisdom. It is an infrastructure of survival — a living sanctuary where healing is communal, sacred, and possible.

The medicine walk

Four movements, one journey

An opening ritual calls the spirit of the plants, the ancestors, and the land. From there, the walk unfolds.

Day One · Online

The Remembering

A global circle of healers, a virtual medicine walk through fields and forests, herbalism master classes, and journeys of cellular restoration.

Walk Day One → Day Two · Online

The Resurrection

From knowledge to embodiment — a farm immersion in the land that heals us back, and wisdom panels from traditions that survived erasure.

Walk Day Two → Day Three · On the road

The Journey

The day you travel to the land — the tending of the nervous system for the road, and a ritual of your own: a seed, a tree, or something you hold dear, brought in honor of someone lost, someone healing, or yourself.

Walk Day Three → Day Four · On the land

The Gathering

Onto the land at dusk — lanterns between the trees, a feast of medicines, drums and dancers, and the honoring of those who heal the healers.

Enter the woods →

A love letter to those who have survived illness

You were never meant to heal alone.

For the ones who know the language of symptoms, who have heard the word diagnosis spoken aloud, who live with the quiet vigilance of survival — this letter is for you.

Read the love letter →
Tickets are open

Where we gather

New York

Get directions →
43.4136° N · 75.3993° W
The Invitation

A love letter to those
who survived illness

Read it slowly. It was written for you.

Beloved,

This is a call to healing. Not the kind that comes only in sterile rooms or printed prescriptions. But the kind that rises from the earth itself. The kind that remembers what our ancestors knew before forgetting became policy. Before healing became industry. Before the language of medicine stopped listening to the wisdom of plants and soil and rivers.

There are ways we have forgotten.

Ways that knew how to cool inflammation with leaves.Ways that knew how to strengthen blood with roots.Ways that knew how to quiet the nervous system with the rhythm of drum and breath.Ways that taught the body how to return.

And now the whisper is rising again. From forests. From farms. From grandmothers who still remember the names of plants. From great-grandmothers whose prayers still hover over our bloodlines.

They whisper across time: You were never meant to heal alone.

This is an invitation. An invitation to step into an infrastructure of survival. A living sanctuary where we begin again. A place where healing is not rushed. Where bodies are listened to. Where symptoms are not dismissed but understood as messages.

A place where we walk with medicine. Where we learn the language of herbs again. Where food returns to its sacred role as nourishment, not harm. Where the soil teaches the body how to regenerate. Where international healers, farmers, herbalists, and physicians gather not to dominate illness, but to outnumber it with wisdom.

A place where we remember that healing is communal. And sacred. And possible.

We call it WILD! Healing.

Not wild in chaos. Wild in the way forests are wild — self-repairing, intelligent, abundant with remedies waiting to be discovered.

Here we resurrect knowledge buried beneath centuries of forgetting. Here we make room for miracles. Here we honor those who have survived illness and those who stand beside them. Here we sit at long tables beneath trees and eat foods that nourish the blood. Here drums echo through the woods and dancers carry the stories of resilience in their feet. Here the healers are honored. And the healers of healers are honored too.

And as the evening lanterns glow between the trees, something ancient happens again. A remembering. You begin to feel it in your chest. A softening. A breath that travels deeper than it has in years.

And perhaps, just perhaps, you hear them. The voices of your grandmothers. The voices of your great-grandmothers. Leaning close to your spirit. Whispering over your life the words that have always belonged to you:

Be well.

So we ask you now — gently, with reverence for everything you have endured:

Are you ready to be well?

If you are — come walk with us. Come walk with the medicines. Come walk toward the miracle of your own healing.

Walk with us →
Online · Sept 9, 10 & 11

The medicine walk begins

You enter through a digital gateway — an opening ritual that calls the spirit of the plants, the ancestors, and the land. A singing bowl rings. A drumbeat begins. The screen opens onto forests, gardens, and medicine fields from around the world. A voice says: "Welcome to the medicine walk."

Sep9
Day One · September 9 · Online

The Remembering

— the ancestral pharmacopeia of the earth

The day unfolds like walking through a forest of knowledge. Each plant is introduced like an elder — not as a commodity, but as a living teacher.

Opening invocation

A global circle of healers

Healers open the gathering with blessings from their traditions.

African herbalists Indigenous medicine keepers Caribbean bush doctors Regenerative farmers Integrative physicians & nutritionists

The Earth has never stopped trying to heal us. We simply forgot how to listen.

The virtual medicine walk

Through fields and forests

A guided digital walk through the Restore Forward land and partner healing gardens across the world. Cameras move slowly through:

fields of moringa beds of lemongrass forests of birch & hemlock rows of healing herbs & regenerative crops
  • how plants regulate inflammation
  • how herbs restore immune surveillance
  • how ancestral remedies resurrect metabolic health
  • how food becomes medicine again

What plant is calling you back to life?

Herbalism master classes

Immersive teachings from international herbalists

The Resurrection Pharmacopeia

How traditional medicine systems used herbs to restore vitality after illness and exhaustion.

Food as Medicine

Healing through:

  • anti-inflammatory foods
  • ancestral fermentation
  • metabolic repair
  • microbiome restoration

Participants cook alongside healers in real time. The kitchen becomes laboratory. Food becomes ceremony.

Healing journeys

The afternoon turns inward

Guided practices of cellular restoration:

nervous system reset breathwork ancestral meditation sound healing

Your body is not broken. It is waiting to resurrect itself.

Sep10
Day Two · September 10 · Online

The Resurrection

— the miracle of repair

Day two moves from knowledge to embodiment. You return to the medicine walk, but now you are asked to see yourself differently — not as a patient, not as a consumer, but as a steward of healing.

Farm immersion

The land heals us back

Travel virtually through the Restore Forward healing landscape and witness:

regenerative farming practices medicinal gardens forest medicine ecosystems healing retreat spaces

Learn how soil health, microbial diversity, and plant medicine directly influence human immunity, mental health, and metabolic repair. When the land regenerates, people regenerate.

The Earth is not just where healing happens. The Earth is the healer.

International healing wisdom panels

Traditions that survived erasure

Healers from around the world share traditions that have survived colonization, industrialization, and medical erasure:

African plant medicine Indigenous nervous system healing Caribbean bush medicine fermentation & longevity diets ritual & movement medicine

Stories of miraculous recoveries. Bodies restored. Communities healed. People rising again.

Sep11
Day Three · September 11 · On the road

The Journey

— the threshold between the screen and the soil

Day three moves from embodiment to passage. This is the journey itself — you travel to the land, body settled for the road, arriving where the gathering unfolds in person on September 12. You have remembered; you have begun to resurrect. Now you set out for the woods — settled, awake, and holding what you chose to bring.

A word for the road

Why the land is medicine

A truth carried down through Indigenous lineage, to hold as you travel: that the land is not a backdrop to healing, but the healer itself.

the land as ancestor the spiritual side of healing grief as medicine reciprocity with the earth

In the days of old, when a person lost their way, the elders would say: go back to the forest. Walk with the medicines. Listen again. Day three is the day you begin to walk.

You do not travel to the land to be healed by it. You travel to remember you were never separate from it.

The bring-something-to-plant ceremony

Choose what you will bring

After all you have received across Day One and Day Two, the journey offers you a ritual of your own. A seed or a tree to plant, or a small thing you hold dear — brought to the land in honor of a name:

a seed, for someone who didn't make it a tree, for someone healing right now a planting, in honor of yourself
  • name the one you carry, and why
  • hold the seed or root as you make the passage
  • let the grief and the gratitude travel with you
  • plant it on the land — a living root left behind

What will you plant, and whose name will you speak into the soil?

Preparing the body for the passage

Keep your nervous system regulated

A pilgrimage is asked of the body as much as the spirit. Before you close the screen and set out, you tend the nervous system so you travel settled, open, and awake:

grounding & breathwork nervous system regulation ancestral prayer for the road setting an intention for arrival

This is not simply a program. It is a pilgrimage — a medicine walk across time and continents. You leave the screen regulated, carrying your seed, ready to arrive not as a guest but as one coming home.

The journey to the land is just as sacred as the land itself.

And then — onto the land · Sept 12

When the virtual journey ends, you are invited to the woods on September 12.

As dusk settles over the forest, lanterns glow between the trees. This is not a gala. It is a ceremony of gratitude.

Enter The Gathering →
The Gathering · In person · Sept 12

Dinner & performance in the woods

As dusk settles over the forest, lanterns glow between the trees. Tables are set beneath the canopy. Wildflowers and branches decorate the space. The air fills with the sound of drums. This is not a gala — this is a ceremony of gratitude.

The gathering, on the land in Ava, New York

The Feast of Medicines

Taste the medicine you have been learning about

The dinner is prepared entirely with healing foods. Each dish tells a story of land and lineage. Food becomes prayer.

  • Regenerative vegetables from the land
  • Medicinal herbs introduced like elders
  • Ancestral grains that carry lineage
  • Fermented tonics for the microbiome
  • Restorative broths to nourish the blood
The Performance

The forest becomes a stage

Drummers begin. Dancers emerge from between the trees. Ancestral movement traditions unfold — African healing dances, Caribbean rhythm ceremonies, Indigenous drum invocations. The performance tells the story of humanity's relationship with the earth.

How we forgot.
How we suffered.
How we returned.

Honoring the Healers

At the height of the evening, the gathering pauses

Names are called. Those who heal the healers are honored — and each honoree receives a blessing. The crowd stands. Drums rise.

community herbalists midwives farmers caregivers physicians elders activists who refused to let their people disappear

Miracles are not accidents. They are what happens when we remember how to live.

The Closing Ritual

Around a communal fire

The lead elder speaks. The invitation is clear: this program is not the end of the journey — it is the beginning. A return to the sacred responsibility of healing ourselves, our communities, and the land.

"In the days of old, people could rise. Not because they were stronger, but because they remembered who they were."

On the land · self-guided

Walk the grounds at your own rhythm

Between the gatherings, the land holds quiet stations — practices to wander into whenever the spirit calls.

Forest bathing

Slow immersion among the trees — letting the woods reach the nervous system.

Fire & drum circle

Rhythm and flame after dark — the oldest medicine for the body and the chest.

Dedicate a tree

Plant and name a tree on the land — a living root left behind for those who come after.

The Mother Tree

Meditation and care at the oldest tree — supporting the one that supports the grove.

Self-guided harvesting

Planting, seeding, weeding — learning stations for hands in the soil.

Grounding station

Bare feet, breath, and earth — a place to remember the body belongs here.

Stargazing

As the lanterns dim, the sky opens — quiet wonder beneath the canopy's edge.

Breathwork & yoga

Self-guided movement and breath in the open air, at your own pace.

Learning stations

Short self-paced workshops scattered through the grounds — medicine walks and more.

Restore Forward · in partnership with The Wellness Studio

Speaker

Dr. Jesse Stoff

MD, MDH, FAAFP · Integrative Physician & Clinical Immunologist

Dr. Jesse A. Stoff is an integrative physician and clinical immunologist who has spent more than three decades focused on the immune system and its role in healing. He earned his BA in biology, cum laude, from Adelphi University, and his MD from New York Medical College in 1981 — followed by postgraduate training that included a family-practice residency year and a fellowship in emergency medicine, and postdoctoral study in clinical medicine and homeopathy at the Royal London Homeopathic Hospital in London, England.

He is founder and Medical Director of Integrative Medicine of New York (formerly Linchitz Medical Wellness) on Long Island, where the work centers on immunotherapy for autoimmune disease, onco-immunology for cancer, and preventative medicine. His approach draws on clinical immunology and the principle of biochemical individuality, integrating conventional and holistic modalities to help restore immune function and the body’s own capacity to heal. He has taught integrative immunology within Dr. Andrew Weil’s Integrative Medicine Fellowship at the University of Arizona and consulted for Cancer Treatment Centers of America.

Beyond his practice, Dr. Stoff founded the Stoff Institute for Medical Research, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit devoted to public education, patient advocacy, and consultation on immune-system disorders and immune reconstitution. He is a Fellow of the American Academy of Family Physicians and a Fellow of the American College of Longevity Medicine, an elected member of the Male Breast Cancer Coalition’s Clinical Advisory Board, and serves on the executive board of the New York Cancer Resource Alliance.

A prolific author, he is best known for the bestselling Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: The Hidden Epidemic and The Prostate Miracle, and more recently the medical text Clinical Immunology, Volume 1: Integrative Onco-Immunology — reflecting a career-long emphasis on integrative, immune-centered care.

  • MD, New York Medical College (1981); BA in biology, Adelphi University
  • Postdoctoral study in homeopathy, Royal London Homeopathic Hospital
  • Fellow, American Academy of Family Physicians (FAAFP)
  • Fellow, American College of Longevity Medicine
  • Founder & Medical Director, Integrative Medicine of New York
  • Founder, Stoff Institute for Medical Research (501c3)
  • Focus: clinical immunology, integrative onco-immunology, autoimmune & preventative medicine
Selected books Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: The Hidden Epidemic · The Prostate Miracle · Clinical Immunology, Vol. 1: Integrative Onco-Immunology
Practice site → ← Back
Speaker

Dr. Dieudonné Jean-Baptiste

MD, MSc, CFMP · Functional & Longevity Medicine

Dr. Dieudonné Jean-Baptiste is a Haitian physician and the founder and lead physician of La Clinique PHI — Priorité Santé Internationale — a functional, integrative and longevity medicine practice in Pétion-Ville, in metropolitan Port-au-Prince. He holds a Doctor of Medicine and a Master of Science in Public Health from the Université de Montréal, and is a Certified Functional Medicine Practitioner (CFMP).

His clinical philosophy is rooted in what he calls la médecine des causes — the medicine of root causes. Rather than treating disease by name, PHI seeks to understand why the body has lost its capacity to repair itself, and to help it recover that capacity. Conventional medicine, as he frames it, excels at managing crises and relieving symptoms; but for chronic conditions such as diabetes, hypertension and persistent fatigue, his work asks why the problem appeared in the first place — with an emphasis on the foundations of health: sleep, stress, movement and nutrition.

At La Clinique PHI, care follows a structured method of evaluation, treatment, reassessment and follow-up, guided by three stated values: scientific excellence, transparency and education, and integrity and empathy. The clinic frames its mission as transforming health through the medicine of causes, under the tagline “La médecine, autrement !” — “Medicine, differently.”

Dr. Jean-Baptiste is an active public voice for functional medicine in Haiti, featured by the Haitian outlet Juno7 and interviewed on LE POINT, the current-affairs program of Métropole Télé, on his root-cause approach to chronic disease and healthy longevity. He also writes for the clinic’s blog, teaching on topics from detoxification to metabolic health.

  • Founder & lead physician, La Clinique PHI (Priorité Santé Internationale), Pétion-Ville, Haiti
  • MD · MSc, Public Health, Université de Montréal
  • Certified Functional Medicine Practitioner (CFMP)
  • Practices functional / integrative / longevity (“root-cause”) medicine
  • Public advocate for functional medicine in Haitian media (Juno7; LE POINT / Métropole Télé)
La Clinique PHI → ← Back
Getting there · Sept 12 · On the land

How to reach the woods

The WILD! Gala — dinner & theater in the woods — gathers on the land at Restore Forward, Ava, New York. However you travel, here's how to arrive.

By air

From your destination city, the nearest airport to listed lodging and the event site is Syracuse Hancock International Airport. From the airport, it's about an hour's drive to Restore Forward.

By car

For navigation, please input this address:
11518 Webster Hill Road, Ava, New York 13309

By train

The nearest Amtrak station is Utica — about a 35-minute drive from the land at Restore Forward.

By charter bus

Catch the charter bus from Brooklyn or Harlem. [Charter bus pickup points, times & booking — to be confirmed]

Sep11
Day Three · September 11 · On the road

The Journey

— the threshold between the screen and the soil

Day three moves from embodiment to passage. This is the journey itself — you travel to the land, body settled for the road, arriving where the gathering unfolds in person on September 12. You have remembered; you have begun to resurrect. Now you set out for the woods — settled, awake, and holding what you chose to bring.

Why the land is medicine

A truth carried down through Indigenous lineage, to hold as you travel: that the land is not a backdrop to healing, but the healer itself.

the land as ancestor the spiritual side of healing grief as medicine reciprocity with the earth

In the days of old, when a person lost their way, the elders would say: go back to the forest. Walk with the medicines. Listen again. Day three is the day you begin to walk.

Draft · The Remembering (copy)
The remembering

In the days of old, when a person lost their way, the elders would say: "Go back to the forest. Walk with the medicines. Listen again."

WILD! Healing is a return to that ancient remembering. It is not simply a program. It is a pilgrimage — a medicine walk across time and continents, guided by healers, farmers, herbalists, and wisdom keepers who still remember how to call the body back to life. Across two days of virtual immersion, you travel a living landscape of ancestral healing, culminating in a third day on the land: a glorious feast and festival in the woods, where the community celebrates the miracle of healing and honors those who heal the healers.
